sub_modules.title: Introduction
sub_modules.content: Pilots rely on instruments to understand aircraft state such as speed, altitude, position, and attitude. These range from simple analogue dials to advanced electronic glass cockpit displays.

sub_modules.title: General Characteristics of Instruments
sub_modules.content: All instruments must balance range, accuracy, resolution, and reliability regardless of their design or complexity.

sub_modules.title: Measuring Range vs Accuracy
sub_modules.content: Instruments must display a wide range while maintaining precision. Higher range often reduces readability of small changes, making accurate interpretation difficult.

sub_modules.title: Linear Circular Scale
sub_modules.content: A linear scale displays values evenly across the dial, where each unit is equally spaced, making interpretation straightforward.

sub_modules.title: Non-linear Circular Scale
sub_modules.content: A non-linear (logarithmic) scale provides better accuracy in critical ranges, such as low rates of climb, by expanding those regions on the dial.

sub_modules.title: High Range Displays
sub_modules.content: When large ranges must be displayed, instruments may use multiple pointer revolutions or multiple scales to improve readability.

sub_modules.title: Multiple Pointer Displays
sub_modules.content: Some instruments use multiple pointers (like a clock) to represent different units such as thousands, tens of thousands, and hundreds of thousands.

sub_modules.title: Ergonomics
sub_modules.content: Ergonomics ensures instruments and controls are designed for easy use, minimizing pilot error and improving interaction between pilot and machine.

sub_modules.title: Instrument Layout
sub_modules.content: Standard layouts like the 'basic six' and 'basic T' organize key flight instruments for efficient scanning and quick interpretation.

sub_modules.title: Analogue vs Digital Displays
sub_modules.content: Analogue displays use pointers and are useful for detecting trends, while digital displays provide precise numerical values for easier reading.

sub_modules.title: Electronic Displays
sub_modules.content: Modern aircraft use glass cockpits with electronic displays. Processing units are often located remotely in avionics bays.

sub_modules.title: Readability and Parallax
sub_modules.content: Instruments must be viewed from the correct angle to avoid parallax error, ensuring accurate reading of values.

sub_modules.title: Colour Coding (Analogue Instruments)
sub_modules.content: Green indicates normal operation, yellow/amber indicates caution, and red indicates danger or limits exceeded.

sub_modules.title: Colour Coding (Electronic Displays)
sub_modules.content: White indicates current status, blue temporary states, green normal, yellow caution, and red warnings in electronic systems.

sub_modules.title: Introduction
sub_modules.content: Aircraft instruments use static and dynamic pressures to determine flight parameters like speed and altitude. Static pressure is ambient atmospheric pressure, while dynamic pressure is caused by aircraft motion through air.

sub_modules.title: Types of Pressure
sub_modules.content: Total (pitot) pressure is the sum of static and dynamic pressure. Dynamic pressure is obtained by subtracting static pressure from pitot pressure.

sub_modules.title: Pressure Relationship
sub_modules.content: Pitot pressure equals static plus dynamic pressure. Dynamic pressure is calculated as pitot minus static pressure.

sub_modules.title: Instruments Using Pitot and Static
sub_modules.content: Altimeter and VSI use static pressure only. Airspeed Indicator and Machmeter use both pitot and static pressures.

sub_modules.title: Pitot Tube
sub_modules.content: A pitot tube faces the airflow and measures total pressure by bringing moving air to rest inside the tube.

sub_modules.title: Static Source
sub_modules.content: Static pressure is measured through vents or ports positioned perpendicular to airflow to avoid dynamic pressure influence.

sub_modules.title: Combined Pitot-Static Probe
sub_modules.content: Some systems combine pitot and static sensors in a single probe with separate channels for each pressure.

sub_modules.title: Pitot Tube Requirements
sub_modules.content: Pitot tubes must be positioned outside boundary layers, aligned with airflow, and equipped with heating to prevent icing.

sub_modules.title: Static Source Requirements
sub_modules.content: Static ports must be positioned to minimize airflow disturbance and accurately measure ambient pressure.

sub_modules.title: Position Error
sub_modules.content: Errors occur due to airflow disturbance around sensors. These depend on aircraft speed, attitude, and probe location.

sub_modules.title: Static Vent System
sub_modules.content: Static vents are placed on fuselage sides where airflow is stable. Dual vents reduce errors caused by yaw or sideslip.

sub_modules.title: Advantages of Static Vents
sub_modules.content: Provide more accurate pressure readings, reduce turbulence effects, and minimize yaw and sideslip errors.

sub_modules.title: High-Speed Probes
sub_modules.content: High-speed aircraft use advanced probes to reduce errors caused by shock waves at high Mach numbers.

sub_modules.title: Manoeuvre-Induced Errors
sub_modules.content: Rapid changes in aircraft attitude cause temporary pressure fluctuations, leading to lag or incorrect instrument readings.

sub_modules.title: Full Pitot-Static System
sub_modules.content: Modern systems use sensors, pipelines or electrical signals, and air data computers to process pressure data.

sub_modules.title: System Configuration
sub_modules.content: Pitot systems are not cross-linked, but static systems are cross-coupled to reduce errors from yaw and sideslip.

sub_modules.title: Alternate Static Source
sub_modules.content: Backup static sources are provided in case of blockage, though they may introduce small errors.

sub_modules.title: Pitot Covers and Static Plugs
sub_modules.content: Protect sensors from dirt and insects when aircraft is parked. Must be removed before flight.

sub_modules.title: Pitot and Static Heaters
sub_modules.content: Heating systems prevent ice formation. Must be checked before flight and used during flight.

sub_modules.title: Preflight Checks
sub_modules.content: Ensure covers are removed, openings are clear, and pitot heater is functioning correctly.

sub_modules.title: Introduction
sub_modules.content: Air temperature is essential for flight safety, performance calculations, and accurate measurement of speed and altitude.

sub_modules.title: Importance of Temperature
sub_modules.content: Temperature affects icing risk, engine performance, air density, airspeed calculation, and altitude accuracy.

sub_modules.title: Icing Hazards
sub_modules.content: Icing can reduce lift, increase drag and weight, freeze controls, reduce engine power, and cause structural damage. Pilots avoid icing by changing altitude or avoiding clouds.

sub_modules.title: Effect on Engine Performance
sub_modules.content: Air temperature affects air density. Dense air allows more fuel combustion and higher power, while less dense air reduces engine performance.

sub_modules.title: Effect on Airspeed Measurement
sub_modules.content: Airspeed depends on air density, which is affected by temperature. Therefore, temperature influences speed calculations.

sub_modules.title: Effect on Altitude Measurement
sub_modules.content: Temperature affects pressure variation with altitude. Incorrect temperature assumptions can lead to altitude errors.

sub_modules.title: Types of Thermometers
sub_modules.content: Air temperature is measured using direct reading (mechanical) or remote reading (electronic) thermometers.

sub_modules.title: Direct Reading Thermometers
sub_modules.content: Use bimetallic strips that bend with temperature changes. Suitable for simple aircraft but not for high-speed aircraft.

sub_modules.title: Remote Reading Systems
sub_modules.content: Use electrical sensors and are suitable for modern aircraft, allowing integration with other systems.

sub_modules.title: Total Air Temperature (TAT) Probe
sub_modules.content: A TAT probe measures air temperature using a platinum resistance sensor. It is positioned outside the boundary layer and includes anti-icing heating.

sub_modules.title: Aspirated Probe (Ground Measurement)
sub_modules.content: Uses bleed air to draw outside air through the sensor when aircraft is stationary, ensuring accurate readings.

sub_modules.title: Sources of Error
sub_modules.content: Errors include instrument imperfections, environmental effects like solar heating and ice, and heating errors caused by airflow.

sub_modules.title: Heating Errors
sub_modules.content: At high speeds, air temperature increases due to kinetic (friction) and adiabatic (compression) heating.

sub_modules.title: Temperature Definitions
sub_modules.content: Static Air Temperature (SAT) is undisturbed air temperature. Total Air Temperature (TAT) includes full heating. Ram Air Temperature (RAT) is the measured value.

sub_modules.title: Ram Rise
sub_modules.content: Ram rise is the increase in temperature due to aircraft speed. It is the difference between SAT and TAT or RAT.

sub_modules.title: Recovery Factor
sub_modules.content: Recovery factor indicates how much of the ram rise is measured by the probe. It is usually less than 1.

sub_modules.title: Temperature Relationships
sub_modules.content: TAT equals SAT plus ram rise. RAT is the measured temperature and is usually less than TAT due to inefficiencies.

sub_modules.title: Conversion of RAT/TAT to SAT
sub_modules.content: SAT can be calculated using formulas, navigation computers, tables, or air data computers.

sub_modules.title: Kelvin Temperature Scale
sub_modules.content: Absolute temperature is measured in Kelvin, starting at absolute zero (-273°C). It is used in accurate calculations.

sub_modules.title: International Standard Atmosphere (ISA)
sub_modules.content: ISA provides standard reference values for pressure, temperature, and density used for calibration and performance calculations.

sub_modules.title: Principle of Operation
sub_modules.content: The ASI measures dynamic pressure, which is the difference between pitot (total) pressure and static pressure. Dynamic pressure is proportional to airspeed.

sub_modules.title: Pressure Relationship
sub_modules.content: Pitot pressure equals static plus dynamic pressure. The ASI subtracts static pressure from pitot pressure to determine dynamic pressure.

sub_modules.title: Dynamic Pressure Formula
sub_modules.content: Dynamic pressure is proportional to air density and the square of true airspeed. This relationship allows airspeed to be calculated.

sub_modules.title: Construction of ASI
sub_modules.content: Static pressure fills the instrument case while pitot pressure enters a capsule. Expansion of the capsule due to pressure difference drives the pointer.

sub_modules.title: Calibration
sub_modules.content: The ASI is calibrated for standard sea level conditions (ISA). Changes in air density with altitude introduce errors.

sub_modules.title: Instrument Error
sub_modules.content: Errors caused by manufacturing imperfections and wear. These are corrected using calibration data.

sub_modules.title: Position Error
sub_modules.content: Errors caused by incorrect sensing of static pressure due to airflow disturbances around the aircraft.

sub_modules.title: Manoeuvre-induced Errors
sub_modules.content: Rapid changes in aircraft attitude cause temporary lag and inaccurate airspeed readings.

sub_modules.title: Indicated Airspeed (IAS)
sub_modules.content: The speed shown directly on the ASI without any corrections.

sub_modules.title: Calibrated Airspeed (CAS)
sub_modules.content: IAS corrected for instrument and position errors. CAS represents true dynamic pressure.

sub_modules.title: Equivalent Airspeed (EAS)
sub_modules.content: CAS corrected for compressibility error. It is the most accurate representation of dynamic pressure.

sub_modules.title: True Airspeed (TAS)
sub_modules.content: EAS corrected for density error. TAS represents the aircraft’s actual speed through the air.

sub_modules.title: Density Error
sub_modules.content: At higher altitude, lower air density causes the ASI to under-read true airspeed.

sub_modules.title: Compressibility Error
sub_modules.content: At high speeds, air compression causes the ASI to over-read. This becomes significant above 300 knots.

sub_modules.title: Error Correction Sequence
sub_modules.content: IAS is corrected for instrument and position error to get CAS, then for compressibility to get EAS, and finally for density to get TAS.

sub_modules.title: Limiting Speeds
sub_modules.content: Important speeds include VNE (never exceed), VNO (normal limit), VFE (flap limit), VS (stall speed), and others related to aircraft performance.

sub_modules.title: ASI Colour Coding
sub_modules.content: White arc: flap range, green arc: normal range, yellow arc: caution range, red line: maximum limit speed.

sub_modules.title: Pitot Blockage Effects
sub_modules.content: Blocked pitot causes incorrect readings. During descent, ASI under-reads; during climb, it over-reads.

sub_modules.title: Static Blockage Effects
sub_modules.content: Blocked static source causes ASI to over-read in descent and under-read in climb.

sub_modules.title: Mnemonic for Errors
sub_modules.content: PUDSOD: Pitot Under-reads in Descent, Static Over-reads in Descent.

sub_modules.title: Pitot and Static Leaks
sub_modules.content: Pitot leaks cause under-reading; static leaks may cause over-reading or unreliable indications.

sub_modules.title: Serviceability Checks
sub_modules.content: Ensure pitot covers removed, sensors clear, heater working, and ASI responds correctly during take-off.

sub_modules.title: Principle of Operation
sub_modules.content: The altimeter measures atmospheric pressure. As altitude increases, pressure decreases. By measuring pressure, altitude is determined.

sub_modules.title: Pressure vs Height Relationship
sub_modules.content: Pressure decreases with altitude, but not linearly. Calibration is required to convert pressure into height.

sub_modules.title: Definitions
sub_modules.content: Height: vertical distance from a reference point. Elevation: height of a fixed point above MSL. Altitude: height of an aircraft above MSL. Pressure Altitude: altitude referenced to 1013.25 hPa.

sub_modules.title: True Altitude
sub_modules.content: The actual vertical distance between the aircraft and the surface directly below.

sub_modules.title: Calibration (ISA)
sub_modules.content: Altimeters are calibrated using International Standard Atmosphere (ISA) conditions for consistent readings.

sub_modules.title: Simple Altimeter
sub_modules.content: Uses a single capsule that expands as pressure decreases. Movement is converted into pointer rotation.

sub_modules.title: Sensitive Altimeter
sub_modules.content: Uses multiple capsules and three pointers (1000s, 10000s, 100000s feet) for better accuracy.

sub_modules.title: Three Pointer System
sub_modules.content: Displays altitude using three needles but can be confusing and prone to misreading.

sub_modules.title: Counter Pointer Altimeter
sub_modules.content: Combines digital counters with a pointer for clearer altitude reading and rate indication.

sub_modules.title: Servo-assisted Altimeter
sub_modules.content: Uses electrical signals and motors to improve accuracy and reduce mechanical errors, especially at high altitude.

sub_modules.title: Variable Datum (Subscale)
sub_modules.content: Pilot sets pressure value (e.g., QNH) using a knob. The altimeter then shows altitude relative to that pressure.

sub_modules.title: Position Error
sub_modules.content: Occurs due to incorrect sensing of static pressure outside the aircraft.

sub_modules.title: Instrument Error
sub_modules.content: Caused by manufacturing imperfections and mechanical friction.

sub_modules.title: Manoeuvre-induced Error
sub_modules.content: Temporary errors due to rapid changes in aircraft attitude affecting pressure sensing.

sub_modules.title: Barometric Error
sub_modules.content: Occurs when incorrect pressure setting is used. Approximately 30 ft error per 1 hPa.

sub_modules.title: Time Lag Error
sub_modules.content: Altimeter response is delayed during rapid climbs or descents, causing under-reading in climb and over-reading in descent.

sub_modules.title: Temperature Error
sub_modules.content: In cold air, altimeter over-reads (true altitude is lower). In warm air, it under-reads.

sub_modules.title: High to Low Rule
sub_modules.content: Flying from high pressure to low pressure or high temperature to low temperature causes the altimeter to over-read.

sub_modules.title: Standard Pressure Setting
sub_modules.content: 1013.25 hPa is used to measure pressure altitude and flight levels.

sub_modules.title: Flight Levels
sub_modules.content: Altitude referenced to 1013.25 hPa, expressed in hundreds of feet (e.g., FL350 = 35000 ft).

sub_modules.title: QFE
sub_modules.content: Pressure setting where altimeter reads zero on the ground. Indicates height above aerodrome.

sub_modules.title: QNH
sub_modules.content: Pressure setting where altimeter reads aerodrome elevation on ground. Indicates altitude above MSL.

sub_modules.title: Regional QNH
sub_modules.content: Lowest forecast pressure in an area, ensuring safe terrain clearance by under-reading altitude.

sub_modules.title: Static Blockage Effects
sub_modules.content: Blocked static source freezes altimeter reading. In climb it under-reads, in descent it over-reads.

sub_modules.title: Static Leak Effects
sub_modules.content: Leaks can cause incorrect altitude readings depending on cabin pressure conditions.

sub_modules.title: Density Altitude
sub_modules.content: Altitude in standard atmosphere corresponding to current air density. Important for aircraft performance.

sub_modules.title: Preflight Checks
sub_modules.content: Altimeter must be checked on ground using known elevation and pressure settings to ensure accuracy.

sub_modules.title: Introduction
sub_modules.content: The VSI indicates the rate of climb or descent in feet per minute by measuring changes in static pressure over time.

sub_modules.title: Purpose of VSI
sub_modules.content: Provides accurate vertical speed information, useful for controlled climbs, descents, and instrument approaches.

sub_modules.title: Principle of Operation
sub_modules.content: The VSI compares current static pressure with pressure from a few seconds earlier to determine rate of climb or descent.

sub_modules.title: Basic Concept
sub_modules.content: In level flight, pressures are equal. During climb or descent, pressure changes occur at different rates due to a restriction, creating a measurable difference.

sub_modules.title: Construction
sub_modules.content: A capsule is connected directly to static pressure, while the case receives static pressure through a restricted choke, creating a time delay.

sub_modules.title: Working in Climb
sub_modules.content: During climb, pressure inside the capsule decreases faster than in the case, causing capsule movement and indicating climb.

sub_modules.title: Working in Descent
sub_modules.content: During descent, pressure increases faster inside the capsule than in the case, causing expansion and indicating descent.

sub_modules.title: Metering Unit (Choke)
sub_modules.content: A calibrated restriction using capillary and orifice ensures accurate rate indication in feet per minute at all altitudes.

sub_modules.title: Display
sub_modules.content: Shows rate of climb or descent, typically in feet per minute, centered at zero for level flight.

sub_modules.title: Instrument Error
sub_modules.content: Caused by manufacturing imperfections affecting accuracy.

sub_modules.title: Position Error
sub_modules.content: Incorrect static pressure sensing can cause false climb or descent indications, especially during acceleration.

sub_modules.title: Manoeuvre-induced Error
sub_modules.content: Rapid attitude changes cause temporary false readings due to pressure fluctuations.

sub_modules.title: Time Lag
sub_modules.content: There is a delay of several seconds before the VSI shows correct vertical speed due to gradual pressure equalization.

sub_modules.title: Static Blockage Effect
sub_modules.content: If static source is blocked, VSI will read zero regardless of actual climb or descent.

sub_modules.title: Instantaneous VSI (IVSI)
sub_modules.content: An improved VSI that uses an accelerometer (dashpot) to provide immediate response to vertical movement.

sub_modules.title: IVSI Working
sub_modules.content: The accelerometer detects initial vertical acceleration and provides instant indication before normal pressure difference builds.

sub_modules.title: IVSI Errors
sub_modules.content: Sensitive to turbulence and may give false readings during turns or unstable flight conditions.

sub_modules.title: Display Types
sub_modules.content: Linear scale shows uniform spacing, while logarithmic scale gives better readability at low climb/descent rates.

sub_modules.title: Serviceability Checks (Ground)
sub_modules.content: Instrument should read zero within allowable limits and show no physical damage.

sub_modules.title: Serviceability Checks (Air)
sub_modules.content: VSI should read zero in level flight and match calculated rate using altimeter and time.

sub_modules.title: Introduction
sub_modules.content: The Machmeter displays the Mach number, which is the ratio of true airspeed to the local speed of sound.

sub_modules.title: Importance in High Speed Flight
sub_modules.content: At high speeds, shock waves can form causing drag, loss of lift, buffeting, and Mach tuck. Mach limits (MMO) must not be exceeded.

sub_modules.title: Mach Number Definition
sub_modules.content: Mach number is the ratio of true airspeed (TAS) to local speed of sound (LSS).

sub_modules.title: Speed of Sound
sub_modules.content: The speed of sound depends on temperature and decreases with altitude as temperature decreases.

sub_modules.title: Speed of Sound Formula
sub_modules.content: Local speed of sound = 38.95 × square root of absolute temperature (Kelvin).

sub_modules.title: Machmeter Principle
sub_modules.content: The Machmeter compares dynamic pressure and static pressure to determine Mach number.

sub_modules.title: Capsule System
sub_modules.content: Uses two capsules: an airspeed capsule (responds to dynamic pressure) and an altitude capsule (responds to static pressure).

sub_modules.title: Working Mechanism
sub_modules.content: Movement of both capsules is combined using linkages to produce a Mach number indication.

sub_modules.title: Ratio Relationship
sub_modules.content: Mach number is proportional to the ratio of dynamic pressure to static pressure.

sub_modules.title: Machmeter Construction
sub_modules.content: Includes airspeed capsule, altitude capsule, ratio arm, ranging arm, and pointer mechanism.

sub_modules.title: Mach Limit (MMO)
sub_modules.content: Maximum Mach number allowed for an aircraft to avoid dangerous aerodynamic effects.

sub_modules.title: Instrument Error
sub_modules.content: Caused by mechanical imperfections in the instrument.

sub_modules.title: Position Error
sub_modules.content: Due to incorrect sensing of pitot and static pressures, especially at high Mach numbers.

sub_modules.title: Manoeuvre-induced Error
sub_modules.content: Occurs due to airflow disturbances during aircraft manoeuvres.

sub_modules.title: No Temperature or Density Error
sub_modules.content: Temperature and density effects cancel out in Mach calculations, so these errors do not affect Machmeter readings.

sub_modules.title: Static Blockage Effects
sub_modules.content: Blocked static source causes Machmeter to under-read in climb and over-read in descent.

sub_modules.title: Pitot Blockage Effects
sub_modules.content: Blocked pitot source causes Machmeter to over-read in climb and under-read in descent.

sub_modules.title: Mach vs CAS vs TAS
sub_modules.content: Mach relates TAS to speed of sound. CAS and TAS vary differently with altitude compared to Mach.

sub_modules.title: Climb at Constant CAS
sub_modules.content: TAS increases and Mach number increases significantly as altitude increases.

sub_modules.title: Climb at Constant Mach
sub_modules.content: TAS decreases and CAS decreases as altitude increases.

sub_modules.title: Descent at Constant Mach
sub_modules.content: TAS increases and CAS increases significantly as altitude decreases.

sub_modules.title: Isothermal Layer
sub_modules.content: In constant temperature layers, speed of sound remains constant, so TAS remains constant at constant Mach.

sub_modules.title: Temperature Inversion
sub_modules.content: In increasing temperature with altitude, speed of sound increases affecting TAS and Mach relationships.

sub_modules.title: Combined Mach/Airspeed Indicator
sub_modules.content: Modern aircraft combine Machmeter and ASI into one instrument, often connected to an Air Data Computer.

sub_modules.title: Air Data Computer (ADC)
sub_modules.content: Processes pitot, static, and temperature data to provide accurate Mach and airspeed readings.

sub_modules.title: Abbreviations
sub_modules.content: MMR: Machmeter reading, IMN: Indicated Mach number, TMN: True Mach number, MMO: Maximum Mach limit.

sub_modules.title: Introduction
sub_modules.content: The Air Data Computer (ADC) replaces conventional instruments by computing altitude, airspeed, Mach number, and vertical speed from pressure and temperature inputs.

sub_modules.title: Air Data System (ADS)
sub_modules.content: The ADC, sensors, displays, and power supply together form the Air Data System, providing essential flight data.

sub_modules.title: Inputs to ADC
sub_modules.content: The ADC receives pitot pressure, static pressure, and air temperature, and sometimes angle of attack (AOA).

sub_modules.title: Outputs from ADC
sub_modules.content: Outputs include altitude, vertical speed, airspeed, Mach number, total air temperature, static air temperature, and true airspeed.

sub_modules.title: Integration with Aircraft Systems
sub_modules.content: ADC outputs are used by systems such as AFCS, transponder, flight data recorder, and navigation systems.

sub_modules.title: Configuration Module
sub_modules.content: Allows calibration of the ADC for specific aircraft characteristics and sensor positioning differences.

sub_modules.title: Analogue ADC
sub_modules.content: Uses continuous signals like pressure and voltage. Mechanical linkages and transducers convert pressure into usable outputs.

sub_modules.title: Digital ADC
sub_modules.content: Uses digital (binary) data. Analogue inputs are converted into digital signals for processing and transmission.

sub_modules.title: Analogue to Digital Conversion
sub_modules.content: Sensors measure physical quantities and convert them into digital signals for processing in the ADC.

sub_modules.title: Computation
sub_modules.content: The ADC continuously solves mathematical relationships between pressure, temperature, and speed to generate outputs.

sub_modules.title: Redundancy
sub_modules.content: Multiple ADCs and independent pitot-static systems ensure reliability and backup in case of failure.

sub_modules.title: Cross-connection
sub_modules.content: ADC systems can be cross-connected so one ADC can supply data to both pilot and co-pilot instruments.

sub_modules.title: Standby Instruments
sub_modules.content: Independent mechanical instruments are provided in case of total ADC failure.

sub_modules.title: Failure Warning
sub_modules.content: Comparison systems monitor ADC outputs and provide warnings through flags or lights if discrepancies occur.

sub_modules.title: Built-in Test Equipment (BITE)
sub_modules.content: Automatic testing system that checks ADC functionality and detects faults.

sub_modules.title: Power-Up BITE
sub_modules.content: Runs checks during system startup, including processor, memory, and sensors.

sub_modules.title: Continuous BITE
sub_modules.content: Performs continuous system checks during operation, typically every second.

sub_modules.title: Maintenance BITE
sub_modules.content: Allows ground crew to diagnose faults using test and history functions.

sub_modules.title: Improved Displays
sub_modules.content: Allows advanced displays such as digital readouts, moving tapes, and integrated instrument panels.

sub_modules.title: Reduced Errors
sub_modules.content: Eliminates mechanical friction and reduces lag errors using electronic processing and servos.

sub_modules.title: Error Correction
sub_modules.content: ADC can automatically correct errors such as position error using programmed calibration data.

sub_modules.title: Centralized Data Source
sub_modules.content: Provides consistent and accurate air data to multiple aircraft systems simultaneously.

sub_modules.title: Clean System Design
sub_modules.content: Reduces pneumatic piping, improving reliability, maintenance, and reducing pressure transmission errors.

sub_modules.title: The Magnet
sub_modules.content: Magnetism is a property of materials like magnetite that allows them to attract iron and align north-south.

sub_modules.title: Magnetic Field
sub_modules.content: The region around a magnet where its influence is felt is called the magnetic field.

sub_modules.title: Poles of a Magnet
sub_modules.content: Magnets have two poles where magnetic force is strongest. Cutting a magnet always results in two poles in each piece.

sub_modules.title: Red and Blue Poles
sub_modules.content: The north-seeking pole is called the red pole and the south-seeking pole is the blue pole.

sub_modules.title: Magnetic Lines of Force
sub_modules.content: Magnetic lines of force flow from the red pole to the blue pole.

sub_modules.title: Attraction and Repulsion
sub_modules.content: Like poles repel each other, while unlike poles attract each other.

sub_modules.title: Magnetization by Stroking
sub_modules.content: Iron can be magnetized by stroking it repeatedly with a magnet in one direction.

sub_modules.title: Magnetization by Alignment and Vibration
sub_modules.content: Aligning iron in a magnetic field and applying vibration or hammering induces magnetism.

sub_modules.title: Magnetization by Magnetic Field
sub_modules.content: Simply placing iron in a magnetic field can induce magnetism.

sub_modules.title: Magnetization by Solenoid
sub_modules.content: Passing electric current through a coil (solenoid) produces a strong magnetic field that magnetizes the material.

sub_modules.title: Demagnetization by Shock
sub_modules.content: Hammering a magnetized object at right angles to the earth’s magnetic field removes magnetism.

sub_modules.title: Demagnetization by Heat
sub_modules.content: Heating a material to high temperatures removes its magnetism.

sub_modules.title: Demagnetization by Alternating Current
sub_modules.content: Applying alternating current in a solenoid reduces magnetism gradually to zero.

sub_modules.title: Magnetic Materials
sub_modules.content: Ferrous materials like iron and steel can be magnetized and affect compasses.

sub_modules.title: Non-Magnetic Materials
sub_modules.content: Materials like aluminium, copper, and plastic do not affect magnetic fields.

sub_modules.title: Hard Iron
sub_modules.content: Hard iron retains magnetism for long periods and is used for permanent magnets.

sub_modules.title: Soft Iron
sub_modules.content: Soft iron is easily magnetized but does not retain magnetism once the field is removed.

sub_modules.title: Earth as a Magnet
sub_modules.content: The Earth behaves like a large magnet with magnetic poles near its center.

sub_modules.title: Magnetic Variation
sub_modules.content: The angle between true north and magnetic north is called magnetic variation.

sub_modules.title: East and West Variation
sub_modules.content: Variation is east or west depending on whether magnetic north lies east or west of true north.

sub_modules.title: Magnetic Dip
sub_modules.content: The angle between the magnetic field and the horizontal plane is called magnetic dip.

sub_modules.title: Magnetic Equator
sub_modules.content: The magnetic equator is where the dip angle is zero.

sub_modules.title: Field Strength
sub_modules.content: The Earth's magnetic force can be resolved into horizontal (H) and vertical (Z) components.

sub_modules.title: Directive Force
sub_modules.content: The horizontal component of the Earth's magnetic field aligns the compass and is called directive force.

sub_modules.title: Effect of Latitude
sub_modules.content: Directive force decreases with increasing latitude and becomes zero at the poles.

sub_modules.title: Secular Change
sub_modules.content: Slow movement of magnetic poles over time causes gradual changes in magnetic variation.

sub_modules.title: Periodic Changes
sub_modules.content: Earth’s magnetism changes daily, annually, and in cycles related to sunspot activity.

sub_modules.title: Magnetic Storms
sub_modules.content: Irregular disturbances caused by solar activity temporarily affect magnetic variation and compass accuracy.

sub_modules.title: Magnetic Compass
sub_modules.content: A magnetic compass indicates direction using the horizontal component of the Earth's magnetic field.

sub_modules.title: Purpose
sub_modules.content: The compass indicates aircraft heading relative to magnetic north.

sub_modules.title: Magnetic Variation
sub_modules.content: The angle between true north and magnetic north is called variation.

sub_modules.title: Compass Deviation
sub_modules.content: Deviation is caused by magnetic interference from aircraft components and varies with heading.

sub_modules.title: Direct Indicating Compass
sub_modules.content: A compass where the pilot directly reads heading from the magnet assembly.

sub_modules.title: Vertical Card Compass
sub_modules.content: A compass with a rotating card attached to magnets, read against a fixed lubber line.

sub_modules.title: Compass Requirements
sub_modules.content: The magnet system must be horizontal, sensitive, and aperiodic.

sub_modules.title: Horizontality
sub_modules.content: Magnets must remain horizontal; achieved using pendulous suspension to counter magnetic dip.

sub_modules.title: Sensitivity
sub_modules.content: Ability of the compass to align with Earth's magnetic field; improved by stronger magnets and reduced friction.

sub_modules.title: Reducing Friction
sub_modules.content: Achieved using jeweled pivots, liquid lubrication, and buoyancy effects.

sub_modules.title: Aperiodicity
sub_modules.content: The compass should settle quickly without oscillation; achieved using damping and low inertia design.

sub_modules.title: Compass Liquid
sub_modules.content: Liquid reduces friction and provides damping; must have low viscosity and stable properties.

sub_modules.title: Liquid Properties
sub_modules.content: Must have low expansion, low viscosity, transparency, low freezing point, and non-corrosiveness.

sub_modules.title: Liquid Swirl
sub_modules.content: During turns, liquid movement can cause additional compass errors.

sub_modules.title: Deviation Measurement
sub_modules.content: Deviation is measured using a compass swing and recorded on a deviation card.

sub_modules.title: Accuracy
sub_modules.content: Compass accuracy tolerance is ±10 degrees.

sub_modules.title: Acceleration Errors
sub_modules.content: Errors caused by inertia acting on the magnet assembly during acceleration or deceleration.

sub_modules.title: Acceleration Error Behavior
sub_modules.content: Maximum on East/West headings, zero on North/South headings.

sub_modules.title: Acceleration Rule
sub_modules.content: Acceleration causes apparent turn toward the nearer pole; deceleration toward the further pole.

sub_modules.title: Turning Errors
sub_modules.content: Errors caused during turns due to displacement of magnet assembly from vertical component of Earth's field.

sub_modules.title: Turning Error Behavior
sub_modules.content: Maximum on North/South, zero on East/West.

sub_modules.title: Turning Through North (NH)
sub_modules.content: Compass is sluggish and under-rotates; pilot must undershoot.

sub_modules.title: Turning Through South (NH)
sub_modules.content: Compass is lively and over-rotates; pilot must overshoot.

sub_modules.title: Sluggish Compass
sub_modules.content: Compass lags behind actual heading change.

sub_modules.title: Lively Compass
sub_modules.content: Compass moves faster than actual heading change.

sub_modules.title: Undershoot
sub_modules.content: Stopping turn early to allow compass to catch up.

sub_modules.title: Overshoot
sub_modules.content: Stopping turn late to compensate for compass over-reading.

sub_modules.title: Effect of Latitude
sub_modules.content: Errors increase with latitude due to stronger vertical magnetic component.

sub_modules.title: Magnetic Equator
sub_modules.content: No dip, so no acceleration or turning errors except minor liquid swirl.

sub_modules.title: Clockwise vs Anticlockwise Rotation
sub_modules.content: Clockwise displacement causes under-reading; anticlockwise causes over-reading.

sub_modules.title: Gyroscope Definition
sub_modules.content: A gyroscope is a rapidly spinning rotor that exhibits properties of rigidity and precession.

sub_modules.title: Gyro Components
sub_modules.content: A gyro consists of a rotor and a spin axis, which defines its orientation.

sub_modules.title: Rotor Speed
sub_modules.content: Aircraft gyros typically spin between 4000 and 55000 rpm.

sub_modules.title: Rigidity
sub_modules.content: Rigidity is the property of maintaining a fixed direction in space unless acted upon by an external force.

sub_modules.title: Gimbals
sub_modules.content: Gimbals allow the gyro to remain fixed in space while the aircraft moves.

sub_modules.title: Degrees of Freedom
sub_modules.content: One gimbal gives limited movement; two gimbals allow full freedom in pitch, roll, and yaw.

sub_modules.title: Precession
sub_modules.content: Precession is the movement of the gyro 90 degrees in the direction of rotation when a force is applied.

sub_modules.title: Precession Behavior
sub_modules.content: The gyro reacts to applied torque at a point 90 degrees ahead in the direction of rotation.

sub_modules.title: Rigidity Factors
sub_modules.content: Rigidity depends on moment of inertia and rotor speed.

sub_modules.title: Moment of Inertia
sub_modules.content: Depends on mass and distribution of mass; higher inertia increases rigidity.

sub_modules.title: Rotor Speed Effect
sub_modules.content: Higher rpm increases rigidity and reduces precession.

sub_modules.title: Rigidity vs Precession
sub_modules.content: Greater rigidity results in less precession; they are inversely related.

sub_modules.title: Gyro Wander
sub_modules.content: Wander is the deviation of the gyro axis from its original position.

sub_modules.title: Drift
sub_modules.content: Horizontal movement of gyro axis.

sub_modules.title: Topple
sub_modules.content: Vertical movement of gyro axis.

sub_modules.title: Real Wander
sub_modules.content: Caused by mechanical imperfections like friction and imbalance.

sub_modules.title: Apparent Wander
sub_modules.content: Caused by Earth's rotation and aircraft movement.

sub_modules.title: Earth Rate
sub_modules.content: Apparent drift due to Earth's rotation, calculated as 15 × sin(latitude) degrees per hour.

sub_modules.title: Earth Rate Direction
sub_modules.content: Negative in Northern Hemisphere and positive in Southern Hemisphere.

sub_modules.title: Transport Wander
sub_modules.content: Apparent drift caused by aircraft movement over Earth's curved surface.

sub_modules.title: Displacement Gyros
sub_modules.content: Measure angular displacement; used in attitude and heading indicators.

sub_modules.title: Rate Gyros
sub_modules.content: Measure rate of rotation; used in turn indicators and yaw dampers.

sub_modules.title: Space Gyros
sub_modules.content: Maintain orientation in space with minimal drift; used in inertial systems.

sub_modules.title: Tied Gyros
sub_modules.content: Corrected continuously to maintain a reference such as magnetic north or vertical.

sub_modules.title: Earth Gyros
sub_modules.content: A type of tied gyro that aligns with local gravity.

sub_modules.title: Tuned Rotor Gyros
sub_modules.content: Traditional spinning disc gyros used in basic aircraft instruments.

sub_modules.title: Ring Laser Gyros
sub_modules.content: Use light beams instead of spinning mass; highly accurate and reliable.

sub_modules.title: Fibre Optic Gyros
sub_modules.content: Advanced gyros using fiber optics; used in modern aircraft.

sub_modules.title: Suction Gyros
sub_modules.content: Powered by vacuum systems; simple but affected by airflow conditions.

sub_modules.title: Electric Gyros
sub_modules.content: Powered by electricity; more accurate and stable.

sub_modules.title: Power Failure Indication
sub_modules.content: Gyros include indicators to show loss of power supply.

sub_modules.title: Gimbal Lock
sub_modules.content: Occurs when gimbals align, causing loss of a degree of freedom and gyro instability.

sub_modules.title: Gimbal Lock Solution
sub_modules.content: Avoided using additional gimbals or gimbal flip mechanisms.

sub_modules.title: Gyros vs Magnetic Compass
sub_modules.content: Gyros provide a stable reference unaffected by acceleration and turning errors.

sub_modules.title: Introduction
sub_modules.content: The Directional Gyro Indicator (DGI) provides a stable directional reference for accurate heading and turns.

sub_modules.title: Non-Magnetic Nature
sub_modules.content: The DGI has no magnetic element and must be synchronized with the magnetic compass.

sub_modules.title: Purpose
sub_modules.content: Provides stable and smooth heading indication without acceleration and turning errors.

sub_modules.title: Complementary Role
sub_modules.content: Works with the compass: DGI gives stability, compass gives true north reference.

sub_modules.title: Basic Principle
sub_modules.content: Uses a horizontal axis gyro that maintains direction in space due to rigidity.

sub_modules.title: Tied Gyro
sub_modules.content: The gyro is maintained in the yawing plane of the aircraft.

sub_modules.title: Gimbal System
sub_modules.content: Inner and outer gimbals allow freedom of movement while maintaining orientation.

sub_modules.title: Axis Orientation
sub_modules.content: Rotor axis, inner gimbal axis, and outer gimbal axis are mutually perpendicular.

sub_modules.title: Working Principle
sub_modules.content: During a turn, the aircraft rotates but the gyro remains fixed, so heading change is indicated.

sub_modules.title: Lubber Line
sub_modules.content: Heading is read using a fixed reference line against the rotating scale.

sub_modules.title: Air Driven System
sub_modules.content: Rotor is spun using air jets powered by suction.

sub_modules.title: Jet Function
sub_modules.content: Jets spin the rotor and help maintain alignment in the yawing plane.

sub_modules.title: Precession Correction
sub_modules.content: If displaced, jet forces create precession to restore correct orientation.

sub_modules.title: Wedge Plate System
sub_modules.content: Fine adjustment system using airflow imbalance to re-erect the gyro.

sub_modules.title: Caging Mechanism
sub_modules.content: Locks the gyro and allows manual alignment with the compass.

sub_modules.title: Caging Purpose
sub_modules.content: Used for synchronization, preventing damage, and re-erecting the gyro.

sub_modules.title: Toppling
sub_modules.content: Occurs when pitch or roll exceeds limits, causing loss of gyro stability.

sub_modules.title: Topple Limits
sub_modules.content: Typically occurs beyond 85° pitch/roll (55° for air-driven gyros).

sub_modules.title: Gimbal Exceptions
sub_modules.content: Certain orientations allow full rotation without toppling.

sub_modules.title: DGI Errors
sub_modules.content: Errors arise due to mechanical, earth-related, and operational factors.

sub_modules.title: Gimballing Error
sub_modules.content: Occurs during bank due to geometry of gimbal system.

sub_modules.title: Random Wander
sub_modules.content: Drift due to mechanical imperfections in the gyro.

sub_modules.title: Apparent Wander
sub_modules.content: Drift due to Earth's rotation and aircraft movement.

sub_modules.title: Earth Rate Drift
sub_modules.content: Drift caused by Earth's rotation, dependent on latitude.

sub_modules.title: Earth Rate Formula
sub_modules.content: Apparent drift = 15 × sin(latitude) degrees per hour.

sub_modules.title: Drift Direction
sub_modules.content: Decreasing in Northern Hemisphere, increasing in Southern Hemisphere.

sub_modules.title: Latitude Nut
sub_modules.content: Used to compensate for Earth rate drift.

sub_modules.title: Latitude Compensation
sub_modules.content: Applies opposite drift to cancel Earth rotation effect.

sub_modules.title: Limit of Compensation
sub_modules.content: Effective only at one latitude; errors occur when moving away.

sub_modules.title: Transport Wander
sub_modules.content: Drift caused by movement across Earth's curved surface.

sub_modules.title: Rotor RPM Errors
sub_modules.content: Variation in rotor speed affects accuracy of compensation.

sub_modules.title: Heading Stability
sub_modules.content: DGI provides stable and smooth heading indication compared to compass.

sub_modules.title: Introduction
sub_modules.content: The Artificial Horizon (AH) provides information about the aircraft’s attitude in pitch and roll, replacing the natural horizon in poor visibility.

sub_modules.title: Purpose
sub_modules.content: Displays aircraft orientation relative to the earth using pitch and bank indications.

sub_modules.title: Display Components
sub_modules.content: Includes a miniature aircraft symbol fixed to the case and a gyro-stabilized horizon bar.

sub_modules.title: Alternate Names
sub_modules.content: Also known as Attitude Indicator or Gyro Horizon.

sub_modules.title: Earth Gyro Principle
sub_modules.content: Uses an earth gyro where the spin axis is maintained vertical using gravity.

sub_modules.title: Rotor Orientation
sub_modules.content: The rotor spins in a horizontal plane, providing stable pitch and roll references.

sub_modules.title: Gyro Axes
sub_modules.content: The gyro has three axes: spin axis (vertical), lateral axis, and longitudinal axis.

sub_modules.title: Pitch Indication
sub_modules.content: Pitch movement rotates the case and outer gimbal, moving the horizon bar relative to the aircraft symbol.

sub_modules.title: Nose-Up Indication
sub_modules.content: When the aircraft pitches up, the horizon bar moves downward relative to the aircraft symbol.

sub_modules.title: Nose-Down Indication
sub_modules.content: When the aircraft pitches down, the horizon bar moves upward relative to the aircraft symbol.

sub_modules.title: Roll Indication
sub_modules.content: Roll causes the aircraft symbol to rotate relative to the fixed horizon bar.

sub_modules.title: Bank Angle Display
sub_modules.content: A pointer shows the angle of bank on a calibrated scale.

sub_modules.title: Limits
sub_modules.content: Typical limits are ±60° pitch and 110° roll for older instruments; modern ones allow greater movement.

sub_modules.title: Toppling
sub_modules.content: Occurs when pitch or roll exceeds limits, causing loss of gyro stability.

sub_modules.title: Re-Erection Time
sub_modules.content: Gyro may take 10–15 minutes to re-stabilize after toppling.

sub_modules.title: Control System
sub_modules.content: Maintains the gyro vertical using pendulous vanes (air-driven) or torque motors (electric).

sub_modules.title: Air-Driven AH
sub_modules.content: Uses suction to spin the rotor and pendulous vanes for vertical alignment.

sub_modules.title: Pendulous Vanes
sub_modules.content: Control airflow to correct gyro tilt using gravity-based sensing.

sub_modules.title: Equilibrium State
sub_modules.content: Equal airflow through all slots keeps the gyro stable with no precession.

sub_modules.title: Correction Mechanism
sub_modules.content: Unequal airflow causes precession that restores the gyro to vertical.

sub_modules.title: Acceleration Error
sub_modules.content: Acceleration affects pendulous vanes causing false pitch and roll indications.

sub_modules.title: Acceleration Pitch Error
sub_modules.content: During acceleration, the instrument shows a false nose-up indication.

sub_modules.title: Acceleration Roll Error
sub_modules.content: Acceleration may cause a false wing-down indication due to inertia.

sub_modules.title: Deceleration Error
sub_modules.content: Opposite of acceleration error, showing nose-down and opposite roll.

sub_modules.title: Turning Errors
sub_modules.content: Caused by centrifugal force affecting pendulous vanes and rotor housing.

sub_modules.title: Turning Error Behavior
sub_modules.content: Errors vary during turns but cancel out after a full 360° turn.

sub_modules.title: Rigidity
sub_modules.content: High rotor speed provides strong rigidity and stable reference.

sub_modules.title: Pre-Flight Check
sub_modules.content: Ensure horizon is level and stable before take-off.

sub_modules.title: In-Flight Behavior
sub_modules.content: Should respond immediately to pitch and roll changes.

sub_modules.title: Electric Artificial Horizon
sub_modules.content: Uses electric motors and levelling switches instead of airflow.

sub_modules.title: Electric Control System
sub_modules.content: Uses mercury switches and torque motors to maintain vertical alignment.

sub_modules.title: Torque Motors
sub_modules.content: Apply force that is precessed to correct gyro orientation.

sub_modules.title: Acceleration Error Reduction
sub_modules.content: Electric AH reduces errors due to higher rigidity and cut-out switches.

sub_modules.title: Cut-Out Switches
sub_modules.content: Prevent false corrections during acceleration and turns.

sub_modules.title: Fast Erection System
sub_modules.content: Allows rapid re-alignment of the gyro using increased motor power.

sub_modules.title: Fast Erection Limitation
sub_modules.content: Must only be used in level flight to avoid incorrect alignment.

sub_modules.title: Adjustable Datum
sub_modules.content: Allows alignment of aircraft symbol with horizon but may be misleading.

sub_modules.title: Vertical Gyro Unit
sub_modules.content: Provides stabilized attitude data to other systems like flight directors.

sub_modules.title: Introduction
sub_modules.content: The Turn and Slip Indicator consists of two instruments: the rate of turn indicator and the slip indicator.

sub_modules.title: Purpose
sub_modules.content: Measures rate of turn and indicates whether the turn is balanced.

sub_modules.title: Rate of Turn Indicator
sub_modules.content: Uses a rate gyro to measure the rate of turn about the vertical axis.

sub_modules.title: Slip Indicator
sub_modules.content: A pendulous device that indicates whether a turn is balanced or unbalanced.

sub_modules.title: Rate Gyro
sub_modules.content: A single-gimbal gyro that measures angular velocity instead of position.

sub_modules.title: Degrees of Freedom
sub_modules.content: The rate gyro has one gimbal and one degree of freedom.

sub_modules.title: Yaw Detection
sub_modules.content: Yaw movement produces a torque that causes gyro precession.

sub_modules.title: Spring System
sub_modules.content: A spring resists gyro movement and its deflection measures the rate of turn.

sub_modules.title: Primary Torque
sub_modules.content: Applied when aircraft yaws, initiating gyro precession.

sub_modules.title: Primary Precession
sub_modules.content: Occurs 90 degrees from applied torque in the direction of rotation.

sub_modules.title: Secondary Torque
sub_modules.content: Generated by spring tension opposing gyro movement.

sub_modules.title: Equilibrium Condition
sub_modules.content: Occurs when spring torque equals rate of turn, stabilizing the gyro tilt.

sub_modules.title: Turn Rate Measurement
sub_modules.content: The tilt of the gyro is proportional to the rate of turn.

sub_modules.title: Rate 1 Turn
sub_modules.content: A standard rate turn of 3 degrees per second.

sub_modules.title: Rate 2 Turn
sub_modules.content: A turn rate of 6 degrees per second.

sub_modules.title: Calibration
sub_modules.content: Instrument is calibrated for a specific true airspeed but errors are small across a wide range.

sub_modules.title: Construction
sub_modules.content: Available as suction-driven or electrically driven instruments.

sub_modules.title: Rotor Speed
sub_modules.content: Lower than other gyros because precession is required for measurement.

sub_modules.title: Damping System
sub_modules.content: Reduces oscillations using mechanical or electromagnetic damping.

sub_modules.title: Gimbal Stops
sub_modules.content: Limits gyro tilt to prevent excessive movement.

sub_modules.title: Toppling
sub_modules.content: Does not occur because the gyro has only one gimbal.

sub_modules.title: Rotor Speed Error
sub_modules.content: Low speed causes under-reading; high speed causes over-reading.

sub_modules.title: Looping Plane Error
sub_modules.content: Occurs in steep turns due to additional precession, causing over-reading.

sub_modules.title: Slip Indicator Construction
sub_modules.content: Usually a ball-in-tube inclinometer filled with liquid for damping.

sub_modules.title: Ball Behavior
sub_modules.content: Acts like a pendulum responding to gravity and centrifugal force.

sub_modules.title: Level Flight Indication
sub_modules.content: Ball remains centered when forces are balanced.

sub_modules.title: Balanced Turn
sub_modules.content: Ball remains centered indicating correct bank for given speed and turn rate.

sub_modules.title: Centrifugal Force
sub_modules.content: Acts outward during a turn and affects the ball position.

sub_modules.title: Slip Condition
sub_modules.content: Occurs when too much bank is applied; ball moves inside the turn.

sub_modules.title: Skid Condition
sub_modules.content: Occurs when insufficient bank is applied; ball moves outside the turn.

sub_modules.title: Turn Coordination Rule
sub_modules.content: Correct turn is achieved when the ball is centered.

sub_modules.title: Rule of Thumb
sub_modules.content: Bank angle ≈ (TAS / 10) + 7 for a rate 1 turn.

sub_modules.title: Instrument Display
sub_modules.content: Needle shows turn rate while ball shows coordination.

sub_modules.title: Practical Use
sub_modules.content: Used to maintain coordinated turns without calculations.

sub_modules.title: Introduction
sub_modules.content: The Turn Co-ordinator is an advanced version of the Turn and Slip Indicator with improved sensitivity.

sub_modules.title: Purpose
sub_modules.content: Indicates both rate of turn and rate of roll (bank initiation).

sub_modules.title: Primary Difference
sub_modules.content: The gyro axis is tilted, making it sensitive to both yaw and roll.

sub_modules.title: Gyro Orientation
sub_modules.content: The gyro is mounted at approximately 30 degrees to the aircraft longitudinal axis.

sub_modules.title: Spring Restrained Gyro
sub_modules.content: The gyro is restrained by a spring which allows controlled precession.

sub_modules.title: Roll Sensitivity
sub_modules.content: The tilted gyro detects banking motion, unlike the basic turn indicator.

sub_modules.title: Turn Initiation
sub_modules.content: Banking the aircraft causes gyro precession, indicating the start of a turn.

sub_modules.title: Indication Method
sub_modules.content: An aircraft symbol moves to show direction and rate of turn.

sub_modules.title: Anticipation Feature
sub_modules.content: The instrument shows roll rate, allowing pilots to anticipate turns.

sub_modules.title: Rate Control
sub_modules.content: Turn rate is controlled by aligning the aircraft symbol with marked graduations.

sub_modules.title: Rate One Turn
sub_modules.content: Standard rate of 3 degrees per second is indicated on the scale.

sub_modules.title: Instrument Variations
sub_modules.content: Different instruments may indicate different turn rates based on design.

sub_modules.title: Slip Indicator Integration
sub_modules.content: Includes a ball-in-tube inclinometer to indicate balanced or unbalanced turns.

sub_modules.title: Balanced Turn
sub_modules.content: A centered ball indicates a coordinated turn.

sub_modules.title: Unbalanced Turn
sub_modules.content: Ball displacement indicates slip or skid.

sub_modules.title: No Pitch Information
sub_modules.content: The instrument does not provide pitch information despite its aircraft-like display.

sub_modules.title: Comparison with Turn Indicator
sub_modules.content: Unlike the basic turn indicator, it responds to both roll and yaw.

sub_modules.title: Operational Advantage
sub_modules.content: Provides earlier indication of turn entry due to roll detection.

sub_modules.title: Display Type
sub_modules.content: Uses an aircraft symbol instead of a simple needle.

sub_modules.title: Pilot Usage
sub_modules.content: Used to maintain coordinated turns and monitor roll rate during maneuvering.

sub_modules.title: Introduction
sub_modules.content: Aircraft magnetism affects compass accuracy by creating deviation from the magnetic meridian.

sub_modules.title: Deviation Definition
sub_modules.content: Deviation is the angular difference between compass heading and magnetic heading.

sub_modules.title: Deviation Direction
sub_modules.content: Deviation is easterly if the compass points east of magnetic north and westerly if it points west.

sub_modules.title: Compass vs Magnetic Heading
sub_modules.content: Compass heading differs from magnetic heading due to aircraft-induced magnetic fields.

sub_modules.title: Compass Swing
sub_modules.content: A procedure used to measure and correct compass deviation.

sub_modules.title: Compass Swing Objectives
sub_modules.content: To determine deviation, reduce errors, and record residual deviation.

sub_modules.title: Reference Compass
sub_modules.content: A high-accuracy ground compass is used as a reference during compass swing.

sub_modules.title: Hard Iron Magnetism
sub_modules.content: Permanent magnetism in aircraft structure that remains constant regardless of heading.

sub_modules.title: Hard Iron Characteristics
sub_modules.content: Produces fixed magnetic fields that do not change with aircraft orientation.

sub_modules.title: Soft Iron Magnetism
sub_modules.content: Induced magnetism caused by external magnetic fields, mainly Earth's field.

sub_modules.title: Vertical Soft Iron (VSI)
sub_modules.content: Induced magnetism due to the vertical component of Earth's magnetic field.

sub_modules.title: Latitude Effect
sub_modules.content: Soft iron magnetism varies with latitude because the vertical component of Earth's field changes.

sub_modules.title: Equator Condition
sub_modules.content: At the equator, vertical magnetic component is zero, so no VSI magnetism is induced.

sub_modules.title: Magnetic Components
sub_modules.content: Deviation can be resolved into components represented by coefficients B and C.

sub_modules.title: Blue Pole Effect
sub_modules.content: A magnetic source in the aircraft can alter compass readings depending on heading.

sub_modules.title: Deviation Pattern
sub_modules.content: Deviation varies with heading and follows sine or cosine curves.

sub_modules.title: Sine Curve Deviation
sub_modules.content: Occurs when magnetic influence is along the longitudinal axis.

sub_modules.title: Cosine Curve Deviation
sub_modules.content: Occurs when magnetic influence is along the lateral axis.

sub_modules.title: Coefficient A
sub_modules.content: Represents mechanical misalignment of the compass and is corrected physically.

sub_modules.title: Coefficient B
sub_modules.content: Represents deviation caused by longitudinal magnetic fields and corrected on east/west headings.

sub_modules.title: Coefficient C
sub_modules.content: Represents deviation caused by lateral magnetic fields and corrected on north/south headings.

sub_modules.title: Correction Method
sub_modules.content: Adjust compass using calculated corrections for each coefficient.

sub_modules.title: Compass Card
sub_modules.content: Displays residual deviation after correction and is placed near the compass.

sub_modules.title: Residual Deviation
sub_modules.content: Remaining deviation after corrections that cannot be eliminated.

sub_modules.title: Graphical Representation
sub_modules.content: Deviation can be represented using curves or tables.

sub_modules.title: Accuracy Limits
sub_modules.content: Direct reading compass must be within ±10°, remote compass within ±1°.

sub_modules.title: Compass Swing Requirement
sub_modules.content: Performed when accuracy is uncertain or after maintenance or modification.

sub_modules.title: Maintenance Conditions
sub_modules.content: Required after component replacement or inspection.

sub_modules.title: Magnetic Disturbance
sub_modules.content: Needed after lightning strikes or major electrical changes.

sub_modules.title: Payload Influence
sub_modules.content: Large ferromagnetic loads require compass recalibration.

sub_modules.title: Shock Impact
sub_modules.content: Compass must be checked after mechanical shock.

sub_modules.title: Latitude Change
sub_modules.content: Compass swing required when operating in significantly different magnetic regions.

sub_modules.title: Storage Effect
sub_modules.content: Long-term parking in one direction can induce magnetism requiring recalibration.

sub_modules.title: Introduction
sub_modules.content: A gyro-magnetic compass combines gyro stability with magnetic reference for accurate heading.

sub_modules.title: Direct Reading Compass Limitations
sub_modules.content: Affected by turning and acceleration errors, cockpit magnetic interference, and cannot feed other systems.

sub_modules.title: Directional Gyro Limitations
sub_modules.content: Eliminates turning errors but lacks magnetic reference and requires manual synchronization.

sub_modules.title: Need for Gyro-Magnetic Compass
sub_modules.content: Combines short-term gyro stability with long-term magnetic correction.

sub_modules.title: System Names
sub_modules.content: Also called Remote Indicating Compass, Slaved Gyro Compass, or Gyro-Magnetic Compass.

sub_modules.title: Main Components
sub_modules.content: Includes flux valve, heading indicator, precession amplifier, precession motor, and horizontal gyro.

sub_modules.title: Flux Valve Function
sub_modules.content: Detects Earth's magnetic field remotely from interference sources.

sub_modules.title: Heading Indicator
sub_modules.content: Displays aircraft heading using a compass card driven by the gyro.

sub_modules.title: Precession Amplifier
sub_modules.content: Amplifies and processes error signals between gyro and magnetic input.

sub_modules.title: Precession Motor
sub_modules.content: Adjusts the gyro position to correct heading errors.

sub_modules.title: Horizontal Gyro
sub_modules.content: Provides stable reference unaffected by short-term disturbances.

sub_modules.title: Operation - Steady Heading
sub_modules.content: Flux valve and gyro alignment produce no error signal when synchronized.

sub_modules.title: Gyro Drift Correction
sub_modules.content: Drift creates an error signal that is amplified and used to realign the gyro.

sub_modules.title: Error Signal Processing
sub_modules.content: AC error signal is amplified, phase detected, and converted to DC.

sub_modules.title: Phase Detection
sub_modules.content: Determines direction of correction for shortest alignment path.

sub_modules.title: Rectification
sub_modules.content: Converts AC signal to DC to drive the precession motor.

sub_modules.title: Operation During Turn
sub_modules.content: Gyro remains stable while flux valve and gyro rotate together, maintaining synchronization.

sub_modules.title: Rapid Synchronization
sub_modules.content: High-gain mode or clutch allows quick alignment during startup.

sub_modules.title: Flux Valve Construction
sub_modules.content: Three-legged magnetic detector mounted remotely, often in wingtip or tail.

sub_modules.title: Flux Valve Principle
sub_modules.content: Uses electromagnetic induction to detect Earth's magnetic field.

sub_modules.title: Faraday’s Law
sub_modules.content: Changing magnetic flux induces an electromotive force in the coil.

sub_modules.title: Three-Leg System
sub_modules.content: Provides accurate heading by resolving magnetic field direction.

sub_modules.title: Rotor-Stator Comparison
sub_modules.content: Compares gyro shaft position with magnetic field to detect error.

sub_modules.title: Null Position
sub_modules.content: Occurs when rotor is perpendicular to magnetic field, producing no signal.

sub_modules.title: Heading Indicator Features
sub_modules.content: Includes compass card, lubber line, heading bug, and warning flag.

sub_modules.title: Heading Selection
sub_modules.content: Pilot sets desired heading using heading bug control.

sub_modules.title: FREE Mode
sub_modules.content: Operates as a directional gyro without magnetic correction.

sub_modules.title: SLAVE Mode
sub_modules.content: Normal mode where gyro is automatically aligned with magnetic input.

sub_modules.title: Manual Adjustment
sub_modules.content: Pilot adjusts heading manually in FREE mode using control switch.

sub_modules.title: Annunciator
sub_modules.content: Indicates error signal activity and system synchronization.

sub_modules.title: Annunciator Function
sub_modules.content: Shows direction of correction and confirms magnetic monitoring.

sub_modules.title: Gyro Drift Types
sub_modules.content: Includes drift (horizontal misalignment) and topple (loss of vertical alignment).

sub_modules.title: Gyro Stabilization
sub_modules.content: Maintained using torque motors and levelling systems.

sub_modules.title: Vertical Alignment
sub_modules.content: Achieved using gravity switches or yaw-axis alignment systems.

sub_modules.title: Precession Control
sub_modules.content: Corrections applied slowly to avoid instability during flight maneuvers.

sub_modules.title: Selsyn System
sub_modules.content: Used to transmit heading data to other instruments.

sub_modules.title: Transmitter Function
sub_modules.content: Converts heading into electrical signals.

sub_modules.title: Repeater Function
sub_modules.content: Reproduces heading at remote displays.

sub_modules.title: System Advantage
sub_modules.content: Provides stable, accurate heading and allows data sharing across systems.

sub_modules.title: Overall Summary
sub_modules.content: Combines gyro stability with magnetic reference to overcome limitations of standalone systems.

sub_modules.title: Introduction
sub_modules.content: INS is a self-contained navigation system using accelerometers, gyros, and a computer to calculate position, velocity, and navigation data.

sub_modules.title: Core Components
sub_modules.content: Includes Inertial Sensor System (ISS), stable platform, accelerometers, gyroscopes, and computers.

sub_modules.title: INS Outputs
sub_modules.content: Provides pitch, roll, heading, track, drift, position, ground speed, and wind data.

sub_modules.title: Waypoint Integration
sub_modules.content: Allows storage of waypoints and computes distance, time, and track error to them.

sub_modules.title: Newton’s Laws
sub_modules.content: INS operation is based on motion laws relating force, acceleration, and velocity.

sub_modules.title: Einstein’s Principle
sub_modules.content: Accelerometers detect changes in velocity, not constant motion.

sub_modules.title: Accelerometer Function
sub_modules.content: Measures acceleration in north-south and east-west directions.

sub_modules.title: Accelerometer Principle
sub_modules.content: Uses a pendulous system where displacement indicates acceleration.

sub_modules.title: Torque Feedback
sub_modules.content: Torque motor restores pendulum to null position and current represents acceleration.

sub_modules.title: First Integration
sub_modules.content: Acceleration is integrated over time to produce velocity.

sub_modules.title: Second Integration
sub_modules.content: Velocity is integrated over time to produce distance.

sub_modules.title: Position Calculation
sub_modules.content: Distance travelled in north and east directions gives latitude and longitude.

sub_modules.title: Gravity Effect
sub_modules.content: Accelerometers must remain level to avoid false acceleration due to gravity.

sub_modules.title: Gyro Stabilized Platform
sub_modules.content: Keeps accelerometers horizontal regardless of aircraft attitude.

sub_modules.title: Integrating Gyroscope
sub_modules.content: Uses viscous damping to measure angular movement.

sub_modules.title: Platform Function
sub_modules.content: Maintains a stable reference frame using gyros and gimbals.

sub_modules.title: Three Axis Control
sub_modules.content: Gyros stabilize pitch, roll, and yaw axes.

sub_modules.title: Earth Rotation Compensation
sub_modules.content: System corrects for Earth’s rotation and curvature.

sub_modules.title: Earth Rate Compensation
sub_modules.content: Depends on latitude and corrects gyro drift due to Earth rotation.

sub_modules.title: Transport Rate Compensation
sub_modules.content: Corrects for aircraft movement over Earth’s surface.

sub_modules.title: Coriolis Effect
sub_modules.content: Accounts for apparent deflection due to Earth’s rotation.

sub_modules.title: Centrifugal Effect
sub_modules.content: Corrects acceleration caused by curved motion.

sub_modules.title: Alignment Process
sub_modules.content: System must be aligned in position, attitude, and heading before use.

sub_modules.title: Warm-Up Phase
sub_modules.content: System components reach operating temperature.

sub_modules.title: Coarse Alignment
sub_modules.content: Initial leveling and approximate heading alignment.

sub_modules.title: Fine Alignment
sub_modules.content: Accurate leveling and heading adjustment.

sub_modules.title: Gyro Compassing
sub_modules.content: Uses Earth rotation to determine true north.

sub_modules.title: Schuler Principle
sub_modules.content: Maintains platform horizontal using Earth-radius pendulum analogy.

sub_modules.title: Schuler Period
sub_modules.content: Oscillation period of 84.4 minutes maintains stability.

sub_modules.title: Bounded Errors
sub_modules.content: Errors that oscillate and return to zero within Schuler cycle.

sub_modules.title: Unbounded Errors
sub_modules.content: Errors that accumulate over time such as drift and distance errors.

sub_modules.title: Inherent Errors
sub_modules.content: Errors due to Earth shape, movement, and system limitations.

sub_modules.title: Mode Selector Unit
sub_modules.content: Controls INS modes such as standby, alignment, and navigation.

sub_modules.title: Standby Mode
sub_modules.content: Used to input initial position data.

sub_modules.title: Alignment Mode
sub_modules.content: System levels and aligns before navigation.

sub_modules.title: Navigation Mode
sub_modules.content: Provides full navigation data during flight.

sub_modules.title: ATT REF Mode
sub_modules.content: Provides attitude and limited heading if navigation fails.

sub_modules.title: Battery Backup
sub_modules.content: Internal battery maintains operation during power loss.

sub_modules.title: Control Display Unit
sub_modules.content: Interface for inputting and displaying navigation data.

sub_modules.title: Waypoint Selection
sub_modules.content: Allows pilot to select and manage navigation waypoints.

sub_modules.title: Track and Ground Speed
sub_modules.content: Displays aircraft track and ground speed.

sub_modules.title: Heading and Drift
sub_modules.content: Displays true heading and drift angle.

sub_modules.title: Cross Track Error
sub_modules.content: Shows lateral deviation from planned route.

sub_modules.title: Track Angle Error
sub_modules.content: Indicates difference between actual and desired track.

sub_modules.title: Present Position
sub_modules.content: Displays aircraft latitude and longitude.

sub_modules.title: Waypoint Position
sub_modules.content: Displays stored waypoint coordinates.

sub_modules.title: Waypoint Zero
sub_modules.content: Represents current aircraft position for direct routing.

sub_modules.title: Distance and Time
sub_modules.content: Shows distance and time to next waypoint.

sub_modules.title: Wind Data
sub_modules.content: Displays wind speed and direction.

sub_modules.title: Desired Track
sub_modules.content: Shows intended track between waypoints.

sub_modules.title: System Test
sub_modules.content: Checks functionality of display and system components.

sub_modules.title: Manual Checks
sub_modules.content: Pilot verifies input data and waypoint accuracy.

sub_modules.title: Error Detection
sub_modules.content: System detects incorrect latitude inputs during alignment.

sub_modules.title: Operator Errors
sub_modules.content: Incorrect waypoint input can lead to navigation errors.

sub_modules.title: System Advantages
sub_modules.content: Provides accurate, self-contained navigation without external signals.

sub_modules.title: Introduction
sub_modules.content: IRS uses laser gyros and accelerometers in a strapdown configuration to provide precise inertial data without mechanical platforms.

sub_modules.title: Technological Advancement
sub_modules.content: Laser gyros eliminate moving parts like gimbals and torque motors, improving reliability.

sub_modules.title: Inertial Navigation Concept
sub_modules.content: Determines position without external references using internal sensors.

sub_modules.title: Strapdown System
sub_modules.content: Sensors are fixed to the aircraft structure and stability is achieved mathematically.

sub_modules.title: Inertial Reference Unit (IRU)
sub_modules.content: Core component providing all inertial outputs to aircraft systems.

sub_modules.title: Primary Outputs
sub_modules.content: Includes pitch, roll, heading, acceleration, velocity, position, and wind data.

sub_modules.title: Derived Outputs
sub_modules.content: Includes flight path angle, acceleration rates, and vertical speed.

sub_modules.title: System Users
sub_modules.content: Used by flight management, flight control, autopilot, radar, and other avionics systems.

sub_modules.title: Primary Sensors
sub_modules.content: Three laser gyros and three accelerometers provide input data.

sub_modules.title: Additional Inputs
sub_modules.content: Requires initial position, barometric altitude, and true airspeed.

sub_modules.title: Laser Gyro Principle
sub_modules.content: Measures angular rate using light frequency differences.

sub_modules.title: Sagnac Effect
sub_modules.content: Frequency difference between counter-rotating light beams indicates rotation.

sub_modules.title: Laser Construction
sub_modules.content: Uses mirrors and gas-filled cavity to create coherent light beams.

sub_modules.title: Triangular Path
sub_modules.content: Laser beams travel in opposite directions along a triangular path.

sub_modules.title: Frequency Change
sub_modules.content: Rotation changes path length, altering beam frequency.

sub_modules.title: Interference Pattern
sub_modules.content: Beam interaction creates fringe patterns detected by sensors.

sub_modules.title: Rotation Measurement
sub_modules.content: Movement of fringe pattern indicates direction and rate of rotation.

sub_modules.title: Angular Rate Sensor
sub_modules.content: Laser gyro acts as a precise angular rate measuring device.

sub_modules.title: Drift Errors
sub_modules.content: Caused by mirror imperfections and noise in the system.

sub_modules.title: Accuracy Factor
sub_modules.content: Accuracy improves with longer optical path length.

sub_modules.title: Lock-In Effect
sub_modules.content: At low rotation rates, beams synchronize causing measurement errors.

sub_modules.title: Dither Motor
sub_modules.content: Vibrates gyro to prevent lock-in and maintain accuracy.

sub_modules.title: Platform vs Strapdown
sub_modules.content: IRS replaces mechanical platform with mathematical stabilization.

sub_modules.title: Microprocessor Role
sub_modules.content: Computes orientation and navigation data in real time.

sub_modules.title: Integration Principle
sub_modules.content: Acceleration is integrated to obtain velocity and position.

sub_modules.title: Gravity Compensation
sub_modules.content: Removes gravity effects from acceleration measurements.

sub_modules.title: Earth Rotation Compensation
sub_modules.content: Corrects for Earth’s rotation at approximately 15.04° per hour.

sub_modules.title: Transport Rate Compensation
sub_modules.content: Corrects for aircraft movement over Earth's surface.

sub_modules.title: Schuler Tuning
sub_modules.content: Maintains stability with an 84.4-minute oscillation period.

sub_modules.title: Automatic Calibration
sub_modules.content: System self-calibrates to maintain accuracy.

sub_modules.title: Alignment Requirement
sub_modules.content: System must be aligned before flight while aircraft is stationary.

sub_modules.title: True North Alignment
sub_modules.content: Determined using Earth’s rotation when stationary.

sub_modules.title: Latitude Input
sub_modules.content: Initial latitude must be entered for accurate calculations.

sub_modules.title: Memory Function
sub_modules.content: System remembers last position and detects input errors.

sub_modules.title: Trihedron Establishment
sub_modules.content: Mathematical process of aligning system axes.

sub_modules.title: No Movement Condition
sub_modules.content: Aircraft must remain stationary during alignment.

sub_modules.title: Fast Activation
sub_modules.content: Laser gyros require minimal startup time.

sub_modules.title: Manoeuvre Resistance
sub_modules.content: System is unaffected by aircraft accelerations and movements.

sub_modules.title: Mechanical Simplicity
sub_modules.content: Fewer moving parts increase reliability.

sub_modules.title: Wide Dynamic Range
sub_modules.content: Capable of measuring a wide range of motion accurately.

sub_modules.title: Low Drift
sub_modules.content: Laser gyros exhibit very low drift compared to traditional gyros.

sub_modules.title: Human Error Factor
sub_modules.content: Most errors arise from incorrect operator input.

sub_modules.title: Introduction
sub_modules.content: Radio altimeter measures aircraft height above ground level (AGL) with high accuracy.

sub_modules.title: Primary Functions
sub_modules.content: Provides height for display, supports automatic landing systems, and feeds GPWS.

sub_modules.title: FMCW Principle
sub_modules.content: Uses Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave to measure distance to ground.

sub_modules.title: Time Measurement
sub_modules.content: Measures time taken for radio wave to travel to ground and return.

sub_modules.title: Frequency Sweep
sub_modules.content: Transmitted frequency varies continuously in a known pattern.

sub_modules.title: Beat Frequency
sub_modules.content: Difference between transmitted and received frequency indicates height.

sub_modules.title: Frequency Averaging
sub_modules.content: Average beat frequency is used to avoid errors during sweep reversal.

sub_modules.title: Operating Frequencies
sub_modules.content: Modern systems operate in SHF band (4200–4400 MHz).

sub_modules.title: Frequency Sweep Rate
sub_modules.content: Frequency varies ±50 MHz about 300 times per second.

sub_modules.title: Antenna Transmission
